title: Task 1: Repair raw-data inspection for ShareGPT-style Hermes traces

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: Task 1 sounds simple on paper: Make the inspection path trustworthy on the real raw dataset before deeper changes.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: And the reason it matters is straightforward: Phase 0 showed that raw Hermes records use `from/value`, but `inspect_traces.py` currently assumes `role/content` and fails on the real file. A broken inspection script undermines every later decision.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: What I like here is that the implementation does not chase a grand redesign. It tightens one seam in the pipeline.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. The implemented result for Task 1 is now marked done in the plan, with concrete verification commands recorded for the repo.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] So what changes downstream because Task 1 is already implemented?

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: This repair makes every later inspection, debugging, and schema decision start from the real Hermes raw format instead of a broken mental model.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: That means the value is not just local correctness. It is leverage for everything that comes after.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Once inspection is trustworthy, it becomes safe to derive metadata and compare raw versus normalized structure without guessing.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [chuckle] This is very home-lab coded: small patch, big increase in trust.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Yes. It follows the same discipline across the whole plan: keep the architecture modest, but make the data and labels harder to fool.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: And because Task 1 is complete, the listener can treat this as a concrete repo change rather than a hypothetical roadmap item.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. This episode is not selling a future intention. It is documenting an implemented improvement and the next layer of value it unlocks.
